title: Bipartite Networks: Two-Mode SRM Analysis
text: preceding vignettes focus unipartite (one-mode) networks every actor can send receive ties. Many networks, however, bipartite (two-mode): senders receivers come different populations. Countries send aid organizations. Students enroll courses. Voters support candidates. settings, SRM decomposes variation sender effects, receiver effects, dyad-specific residuals — covariance components (reciprocity, actor-partner covariance) defined actor appears sides. vignette walks bipartite SRM workflow using Game Thrones-inspired dataset, demonstrates simulation-based validation longitudinal two-mode analysis.

title: Bipartite Networks: Two-Mode SRM Analysis
text: small_council dataset 10 x 7 matrix simulated “pursuit intensity” scores. row Great House Westeros; column Small Council position. value cell (,j)(, j) represents aggressively House ii pursues position jj, 0–10 scale. rows (Houses) columns (positions) distinct populations — House position vice versa. makes network bipartite.

title: Bipartite Networks: Two-Mode SRM Analysis
text: Pass rectangular matrix directly srm(). function detects non-square shape applies bipartite decomposition automatically. Three variance components reported. Actor variance (37.8%) captures differences overall ambition across Houses — Houses pursue positions aggressively others largely passive. Partner variance (28.9%) captures desirable position average — Hand King sought Master Laws. Unique variance (33.2%) captures house-position affinities explained either side’s general tendency. covariance components appear reciprocity actor-partner covariance defined senders receivers belong different populations.  roughly even three-way split means network structure driven combination Houses ambitious, positions attractive, specific House-position pairings deviate general patterns.
code: fit = srm(small_council) fit Social Relations Model ----------------------------------------  Mode:       bipartite Actors:     10 Grand mean: 5.4357  Variance Decomposition:   Actor          2.0486  ( 37.8%)   Partner        1.5662  ( 28.9%)   Unique         1.7980  ( 33.2%) plot(fit, type = "variance")

title: SRM Methodology: Mathematical Framework
text: Social Relations Model (SRM) originally developed Kenny La Voie (1984) round-robin designs psychology, every individual rates rated every individual. framework decomposes observed relational data additive components capture systematic patterns actor level relationship-specific patterns dyadic level. srm package implements SRM decomposition network data, extending applicability political science, international relations, domains relational data arises naturally.

title: SRM Methodology: Mathematical Framework
text: directed network nn actors, let XijX_{ij} denote observed tie actor ii actor jj. SRM decomposes : Xij=μ+ai+bj+gijX_{ij} = \mu + a_i + b_j + g_{ij} : μ\mu grand mean network aia_i actor effect individual ii (sender tendency) bjb_j partner effect individual jj (receiver tendency) gijg_{ij} unique (dyadic) effect →ji \j relationship

title: SRM Methodology: Mathematical Framework
text: Actor effect (aia_i): much actor ii sends ties network average, adjusting partner characteristics. high positive aia_i means actor ii unusually active sender. Partner effect (bjb_j): much actor jj receives ties network average, adjusting actor characteristics. high positive bjb_j means actor jj unusually attractive receiver. Unique effect (gijg_{ij}): component →ji \j tie explained ii’s general sending tendency jj’s general receiving tendency. captures relationship-specific dynamics.

text: key summary SRM variance partition, expresses proportion total network variation attributable component: %Actor=σ̂a2σ̂a2+σ̂b2+σ̂g2×100\text{\% Actor} = \frac{\hat{\sigma}^2_a}{\hat{\sigma}^2_a + \hat{\sigma}^2_b + \hat{\sigma}^2_g} \times 100 tells us whether network structure primarily driven differences sending behavior (actor effects), receiving behavior (partner effects), relationship-specific factors (unique effects).

title: SRM Methodology: Mathematical Framework
text: SRM uses method--moments estimation, variance component estimates constrained non-negative. small networks (roughly n<15n < 15), sampling variability can produce negative estimates σ̂a2\hat{\sigma}^2_a σ̂b2\hat{\sigma}^2_b. negative estimate mean true variance negative — means data contain enough information estimate component reliably. computing variance partitions, negative estimates clipped zero calculating percentages. Negative estimates common one component dominates (e.g., nearly variation dyadic) network small. larger networks, estimators stabilize negative values become rare. encounter negative variance estimates, treat decomposition exploratory rather definitive.

title: SRM Methodology: Mathematical Framework
text: sampling distributions SRM variance components straightforward, srm package provides permutation-based inference via permute_srm(). procedure: Compute observed variance components original matrix. Repeatedly permute rows columns matrix independently, destroying actor/partner structure preserving marginal distribution. Recompute variance components permuted matrix. Compare observed values null distribution obtain p-values. permutation test confirms actor variance partner variance significantly larger expected random relabeling (p<0.05p < 0.05). relationship covariance also significant (p<0.05p < 0.05), confirming genuine reciprocity friendship ratings. unique variance significant null (p=1.0p = 1.0) permutation preserves overall variability destroys structure. actor-partner covariance significant (p>0.8p > 0.8), meaning Queen Bee pattern strong enough rule chance sample size.

text: can visualize actor, partner, dyadic effects using srm_plot function. actor/partner effect plots show actors sorted absolute magnitude effects, positive effects dark bars negative effects light gray bars. default number actors shown top 10, adjustable via n argument. Things note: Users need put results srm_effects function input srm_plot function. undirected networks, use type = "actor" plotting since partner effects identical. look actor effects cross-sectional data:  Positive effects shown dark bars (right side) negative effects light gray bars (left side). China highest positive effect, engaging military consultation obligations significantly network average. Countries Turkmenistan strongest negative effects. can also look dyadic effects. users supply unique effects, function returns heatmap diverging color scale. Dark cells indicate stronger--expected relationships (positive unique effects), blue cells indicate weaker--expected relationships (negative unique effects), white cells close predict actor tendencies alone.  heatmap reveals bilateral relationships unusually strong weak controlling actor’s general tendencies. example, USA strong military consultation relationships Japan, Korea, Thailand beyond general activity levels predict. cells white, showing many bilateral relationships close expect. longitudinal data, can set facet = TRUE compare actor effects across time periods:  faceted plot shows actor effects evolve time. China consistently highest positive actor effect. India’s effect grows across periods new consultation agreements enter data. countries light gray bars negative effects, suggesting -average consultation activity.

title: Social Relations Models for Network Analysis
text: examples use original component functions (srm_effects, srm_stats, srm_plot). package also provides unified srm() function computes everything , returning single object print, summary, plot methods. srm() approach concise typical analysis: symmetric (undirected) network, variance decomposition heavily constrained: actor partner variance equal (9%), unique variance dominates 82%. relationship covariance equals unique variance actor-partner covariance equals actor variance, construction. SRM distinguish sender behavior receiver behavior .  lopsided 9% / 9% / 82% split typical symmetric networks reflects structural constraints rather substantive finding. directed networks, decomposition far informative. classroom dataset (directed friendship network) produces 42% / 26% / 32% split across distinct actor, partner, unique components, sender tendencies dominating. See pipeline vignette complete directed-data walkthrough.

title: Social Relations Model Toolkit for Network Analysis
text: srm R package decomposing network data using Social Relations Model (SRM). partitions variation directed networks three components: actor effects (sender tendencies), partner effects (receiver tendencies), unique dyadic effects (relationship-specific deviations). also estimates covariance structures capture reciprocity correlation sending receiving behavior. Part netify ecosystem network analysis R.

title: Permutation test for SRM variance components — permute_srm
text: mat square matrix, list square matrices, netify object, object class srm. Bipartite (rectangular) networks supported; error raised input non-square. n_perms Number permutations (default 1000). time Optional time points subset . seed Optional random seed reproducibility.

title: Permutation test for SRM variance components — permute_srm
text: object class srm_permtest containing: observed Named vector observed variance components. perm_dist Matrix permuted variance components (n_perms x components). p_values One-tailed p-values component. n_perms Number permutations used.

title: Permutation test for SRM variance components — permute_srm
text: permutation, rows columns adjacency matrix independently permuted, destroying actor partner structure preserving marginal distribution. SRM variance components recomputed permuted matrix observed values compared null distribution. Interpreting unique variance p-values: Permutation typically increases dyadic noise, observed unique variance usually smaller null distribution. p-value 1.0 unique variance expected imply dyadic effects absent.

title: Simulate network data from a Social Relations Model — sim_srm
code: sim_srm(   n_actors = 10,   n_time = 1,   actor_var = 1,   partner_var = 1,   unique_var = 1,   actor_partner_cov = 0,   relationship_cov = 0,   grand_mean = 0,   bipartite = FALSE,   seed = NULL )

title: Simulate network data from a Social Relations Model — sim_srm
text: n_actors Number actors (nodes) network. bipartite networks, length-2 vector c(n_row, n_col) specifying number senders receivers. n_time Number time periods (default 1 cross-sectional). actor_var Variance actor (sender) effects. partner_var Variance partner (receiver) effects. unique_var Variance unique dyadic effects. actor_partner_cov Covariance actor partner effects. Must satisfy actor_partner_cov^2 <= actor_var * partner_var. relationship_cov Covariance unique effects within dyads (generalized reciprocity). Must satisfy abs(relationship_cov) <= unique_var. grand_mean Overall network mean. bipartite TRUE, generate bipartite (two-mode) network. bipartite, n_actors length-2 vector c(n_row, n_col). actor_partner_cov relationship_cov parameters ignored bipartite networks (quantities defined senders receivers different populations). seed Optional random seed reproducibility.

title: Simulate network data from a Social Relations Model — sim_srm

text: object class srm, list containing: actor_effects Actor (sender) effects time point. partner_effects Partner (receiver) effects time point. unique_effects Unique dyadic effects time point. stats data frame variance / covariance components time point. grand_mean overall network mean time point. matrices (possibly time-filtered) input matrices used. n_time Number time points. n_actors Number actors (per time point). bipartite Logical; whether model fit bipartite.

title: Fit a Social Relations Model to network data — srm
text: SRM decomposes observation \(X_{ij}\) : $$X_{ij} = \mu + a_i + b_j + g_{ij}$$ \(\mu\) grand mean, \(a_i\) actor (sender) effect, \(b_j\) partner (receiver) effect, \(g_{ij}\) unique dyadic effect. unipartite (square) networks, five variance/covariance components estimated: actor variance, partner variance, unique variance, relationship covariance (generalized reciprocity), actor-partner covariance. require least 4 actors (n >= 4). n = 3, effects estimated variance components returned NA. SRM uses method--moments estimation, variance estimates constrained non-negative. Negative estimates can occur small networks (roughly n < 15) indicate component estimated reliably. computing variance partitions, negative estimates clipped zero calculating percentages. bipartite (rectangular) networks, actor variance, partner variance, unique variance estimated (covariance components defined senders receivers come different populations). Effects simple deviations grand mean. Variance components bias-corrected using two-way ANOVA degrees freedom. minimum size restriction beyond n >= 2 mode. Symmetric networks: input matrix symmetric (undirected), actor partner effects identical construction. variance decomposition constrained accordingly. SRM informative directed (asymmetric) networks.

title: Fit a Social Relations Model to network data — srm
code: mat = matrix(c(0, 1, 2, 2, 0, 1, 1, 2, 0), 3, 3) rownames(mat) = colnames(mat) = c("A", "B", "C") fit = srm(mat) fit #> Social Relations Model #> ----------------------------------------  #> Mode:       unipartite #> Actors:     3 #> Grand mean: 1.5000 #>  #> Variance Decomposition: #>   (Not estimable -- network too small, need n >= 4)  data(classroom) fit = srm(classroom) fit #> Social Relations Model #> ----------------------------------------  #> Mode:       unipartite #> Actors:     12 #> Grand mean: 3.5077 #>  #> Variance Decomposition: #>   Actor          1.4000  ( 42.3%) #>   Partner        0.8539  ( 25.8%) #>   Unique         1.0546  ( 31.9%) #>   Relationship   0.6885  (cov) #>   Actor-Partner  -0.2525  (cov) summary(fit) #> Social Relations Model - Variance Decomposition #> ==================================================  #>  #> Component                Variance  % Total #> ------------------------------------------  #> Actor                      1.4000    42.3% #> Partner                    0.8539    25.8% #> Unique                     1.0546    31.9% #> Relationship (cov)         0.6885       -- #> Actor-Partner (cov)       -0.2525       --

text: mat square numeric matrix (list square matrices longitudinal data). Diagonal values set zero internally. NAs replaced zero. Must srm object (statistics already summary(fit)$variance_table). type One : "rowmeans": row means (average -ties per actor). "colmeans": column means (average -ties per actor). "totalmeans": grand mean -diagonal entries. "actor_var": actor (sender) variance component. "partner_var": partner (receiver) variance component. "unique_var": unique dyadic variance component. title: srm 1.0.0
text: S3 class system: New srm() function serves main entry point, returning object class srm print(), summary(), plot() methods. Variance decomposition: summary.srm() produces full variance partition table showing actor, partner, unique variance components percentages. Variance partition plot: plot(fit, type = "variance") creates bar chart variance proportions. Simulation: sim_srm() generates synthetic network data known SRM parameters validation, teaching, power analysis. Supports cross-sectional, longitudinal, bipartite designs. Permutation inference: permute_srm() provides permutation-based tests whether variance components significantly differ zero, print() plot() methods results. Bipartite (two-mode) networks: functions now support rectangular (bipartite) adjacency matrices senders receivers come different populations. Longitudinal analysis: srm_trends() extracts actor/partner effects time tidy data frame. srm_trend_plot() visualizes temporal trajectories. srm_stability() computes rank-order correlations consecutive time points. Improved netify integration: srm() directly accepts netify objects (cross-sectional longitudinal) without manual conversion. Additional datasets: classroom: Simulated 12-student friendship ratings. trade_net: Simulated 10-country trade network 3 periods.
